title: Transferring a design for pyrography
subtitle: Carbon paper, graphite transfer, and the free-draw method
excerpt: Getting a design onto wood before burning is the step that determines whether the finished piece follows the intended pattern. Three methods work for different design types: carbon-paper transfer for detailed tracings, graphite rubbing for simple shapes, and free drawing directly on the wood surface.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for decoration-transfer methods on wood surfaces.

content.text: work begins, the design needs to be on the wood surface in a form the burner can follow. Burning a complex design freehand without guidelines produces uncertain results; a transferred outline gives a clear path. The method chosen depends on the design complexity and the wood preparation.

Sand the surface to 240 grit before transferring a design. A rough surface breaks the transfer line and makes the burning line uneven. Wipe off any dust with a dry cloth; no water. The surface should be clean, dry, and pale. The paler and more even the wood, the more clearly the transfer will show and the more contrast the burned design will have.

Print or draw the design at the intended size on paper. Place a sheet of graphite carbon paper face-down on the sanded wood surface. Lay the design on top of the carbon paper. Tape all three layers to the workpiece at the corners. Trace every line of the design firmly with a pencil or a dry ballpoint pen. Lift the design and carbon paper away. The graphite lines transferred to the wood will be faint but visible.

Graphite carbon paper (not the blue or black office-copy carbon used in duplicate forms) is the correct product. The graphite burns away cleanly as the tip passes over it and leaves no residue in the finished burn. Blue or black carbon paper leaves permanent coloured marks if any transfer line is missed by the burner.

For simple shapes without detailed line work, scribble heavily on the back of the printed design with a soft pencil (4B or 6B). Turn the design face-up, position it on the wood, and tape it in place. Trace the outlines firmly. The graphite on the back of the paper transfers directly to the wood surface as you trace. This method works well for organic shapes, leaf outlines, and simple repeating motifs.
Method 3: Free drawing on the wood
For geometric or freehand designs, draw directly on the sanded wood surface with a light pencil line. Use a ruler and dividers for repeating geometric patterns. The pencil line will burn away as the
follows it. Any pencil that is not burned over can be erased after the piece has cooled completely, using a soft eraser on the unburned areas.

Set the burner to a medium temperature before beginning. A temperature that is too high will produce a thick, overburned line on the first pass; too low means the tip drags and produces an uneven line. Test on scrap of the same wood species before working the final piece. Follow the transferred line with the writing tip in a smooth, even motion. Slower movement produces a darker, deeper burn. Faster movement produces a lighter one. For the first outline pass, work at a consistent pace that produces a mid-tone line; darker shading is added in a second pass after the full outline is in.

attrs.intro: Design transfer problems and their fixes.

items.fix: Flip the carbon paper to ensure the graphite side faces the wood surface. Trace with firm, consistent pressure.
items.cause: The carbon paper was placed face-up instead of face-down, or the pencil pressure was too light during tracing.
items.symptom: The transferred graphite lines are barely visible on the wood

items.fix: Keep hand speed consistent throughout each line. If the temperature of a solid-tip burner drifts, wait a moment before continuing; solid-tip units take a few minutes to stabilise after switching on.
items.cause: The burner temperature changed, or the hand speed varied significantly between sections.
items.symptom: The burning line is much darker on some sections than others

items.fix: Clean the smeared areas with a barely damp cloth and allow to dry. Tape all layers firmly at multiple corners before tracing. Work from the centre of the design outward to avoid hand-pressure-driven movement.
items.cause: The carbon paper or graphite rubbing was not taped securely enough and slipped during tracing.
items.symptom: The transfer lines have smeared across the wood surface beyond the design outline

attrs.heading: Common problems
